title: Amiel Weeks Whipple

[United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army")

(October 21, 1817 – May 7, 1863)[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1) was an American military officer and [topographical engineer](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Topographical_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Topographical Engineers"). He served as a [brigadier general](/wiki/Brigadier_general_(United_States) "Brigadier general (United States)") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), where he was mortally wounded at the [Battle of Chancellorsville](/wiki/Battle_of_Chancellorsville "Battle of Chancellorsville"). Among his many survey assignments for the US War Department, he participated in the difficult survey of the new [United States and Mexico boundary](/wiki/United_States_and_Mexican_Boundary_Survey "United States and Mexican Boundary Survey") and led the survey of a possible [transcontinental railroad route](/wiki/Pacific_Railroad_Survey "Pacific Railroad Survey") along the [35th parallel](/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_30th_parallel_north_and_the_35th_parallel_north#35th_parallel_north "Circles of latitude between the 30th parallel north and the 35th parallel north") from Arkansas to Los Angeles.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American military officer and topographical engineer (1817–1863)

| Amiel Weeks Whipple | |
| --- | --- |
| Amiel Weeks Whipple | |

| Died | May 7, 1863(1863-05-07) (aged 44)  [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") |
| Place of burial | Proprietors' Cemetery, [Portsmouth, New Hampshire](/wiki/Portsmouth,_New_Hampshire "Portsmouth, New Hampshire") |
| Allegiance | [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") |
| Branch | [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") |
| Service years | 1841–1863 |
| Rank | [Major General](/wiki/Major_general_(United_States) "Major general (United States)") |
| Conflicts | [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") |

**Amiel Weeks Whipple** (October 21, 1817 – May 7, 1863)[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1) was an American military officer and [topographical engineer](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Topographical_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Topographical Engineers"). He served as a [brigadier general](/wiki/Brigadier_general_(United_States) "Brigadier general (United States)") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), where he was mortally wounded at the [Battle of Chancellorsville](/wiki/Battle_of_Chancellorsville "Battle of Chancellorsville"). Among his many survey assignments for the US War Department, he participated in the difficult survey of the new [United States and Mexico boundary](/wiki/United_States_and_Mexican_Boundary_Survey "United States and Mexican Boundary Survey") and led the survey of a possible [transcontinental railroad route](/wiki/Pacific_Railroad_Survey "Pacific Railroad Survey") along the [35th parallel](/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_30th_parallel_north_and_the_35th_parallel_north#35th_parallel_north "Circles of latitude between the 30th parallel north and the 35th parallel north") from Arkansas to Los Angeles.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amiel_Weeks_Whipple&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Whipple was born to David and Abigail Brown (Pepper) Whipple in [Greenwich, Massachusetts](/wiki/Greenwich,_Massachusetts "Greenwich, Massachusetts"). He grew up in [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts"), where his father owned an inn. He was teaching school at Concord in 1834 when he applied to the [United States Military Academy at West Point](/wiki/United_States_Military_Academy_at_West_Point "United States Military Academy at West Point"). After he was turned down by the academy, he attended [Amherst College](/wiki/Amherst_College "Amherst College") until 1837, when he was finally accepted to West Point. Whipple graduated fifth in the class of 1841. His early career included surveying the [Patapsco River](/wiki/Patapsco_River "Patapsco River"), sounding and mapping the approaches to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), and surveying [Portsmouth Harbor](/wiki/Portsmouth_Harbor "Portsmouth Harbor"). From 1844 to 1849, he was assigned to a survey of the northeastern boundary of the United States, serving under [James Duncan Graham](/wiki/James_Duncan_Graham "James Duncan Graham").[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DAB-2)

In 1848, Whipple was assigned to the United States Boundary Commission, tasked with [surveying the new boundary](/wiki/United_States_and_Mexican_Boundary_Survey "United States and Mexican Boundary Survey") between the US and Mexico resulting from the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"). For a time, he served as the interim chief surveyor for the commission until [William H. Emory](/wiki/William_H._Emory "William H. Emory") was named to the position. Heading east from San Diego, the survey parties traveled through some of the most rugged and remote terrain in the country. Compounding the difficulty was the extreme desert heat and hostile [Apache Indians](/wiki/Apache_Indians "Apache Indians"). Whipple accomplished his assigned surveys in spite of these challenges and was promoted to first lieutenant on April 24, 1851.[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1) By 1853, his survey work was complete and his reports had been written.[[3]](#cite_note-Werne-3)

In 1853, Congress authorized the [Pacific Railroad Surveys](/wiki/Pacific_Railroad_Surveys "Pacific Railroad Surveys") to select the best route for a transcontinental railroad. Whipple was directed to lead one of those surveys along the 35th parallel from [Fort Smith, Arkansas](/wiki/Fort_Smith,_Arkansas "Fort Smith, Arkansas"), to Los Angeles. The Whipple expedition left Fort Smith on July 15, 1853, with a group of seventy men, including soldiers, teamsters, and scientists. Among the scientists were [John Milton Bigelow](/wiki/John_Milton_Bigelow "John Milton Bigelow"), a medical doctor and botanist; [Jules Marcou](/wiki/Jules_Marcou "Jules Marcou"), a Swiss geologist; and [Balduin Möllhausen](/wiki/Balduin_M%C3%B6llhausen "Balduin Möllhausen"), a German artist and a protege of [Alexander von Humboldt](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt "Alexander von Humboldt"). Whipple's party made good progress through [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") (now Oklahoma), the Texas panhandle and the [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory"), reaching [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico") on October 5.[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1)[[4]](#cite_note-McDougall-4) After Albuquerque, they were joined by frontiersman [Antoine Leroux](/wiki/Antoine_Leroux "Antoine Leroux") who helped to guide the surveyors through the most difficult part of the journey to California. They reached California on February 7 after a near-disastrous crossing of the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River"), then crossed the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") and reached Los Angeles on March 20, 1854.[[5]](#cite_note-Goetz-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Beid-6)

The findings of the Whipple expedition, along with those of the other transcontinental survey parties, were published by the government in a massive twelve-volume report titled the *Pacific Railroad Reports*. In addition to reports on topography, geology, botany and zoology, Whipple wrote a lengthy essay on the southwestern Indian tribes that the expedition had encountered.[[5]](#cite_note-Goetz-5)

Whipple was promoted to captain in 1855 and then assigned to supervise efforts to open the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") to navigation by larger vessels, deepening channels through the [St. Clair flats](/wiki/St._Clair_River "St. Clair River") and the [St. Marys River](/wiki/St._Marys_River_(Michigan-Ontario) "St. Marys River (Michigan-Ontario)"). He was also commander of the lighthouse districts from [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") to the [St. Lawrence River](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River").[[6]](#cite_note-Beid-6)

At the outbreak of the Civil War, Whipple served for a time under General [Irvin McDowell](/wiki/Irvin_McDowell "Irvin McDowell") as commander of the nascent [balloon reconnaissance](/wiki/Observation_balloon "Observation balloon") unit.[[6]](#cite_note-Beid-6) He made a balloon ascent over the Confederate lines at [Bull Run](/wiki/Bull_Run_(Occoquan_River) "Bull Run (Occoquan River)").[[5]](#cite_note-Goetz-5) He then became chief topographical engineer under General [George B. McClellan](/wiki/George_B._McClellan "George B. McClellan") in the [Army of the Potomac](/wiki/Army_of_the_Potomac "Army of the Potomac"). His maps were used on many Virginia battlefields. In 1862, as brigadier general of volunteers, he led the defense of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), on its Virginia side. In September 1862, Whipple was assigned to the [III Corps](/wiki/III_Corps_(Union_Army) "III Corps (Union Army)"), and on December 13–15, participated in the [Battle of Fredericksburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Fredericksburg "Battle of Fredericksburg"). Whipple was severely wounded by a sharpshooter at [Chancellorsville](/wiki/Battle_of_Chancellorsville "Battle of Chancellorsville") on May 4, 1863, received last rites on the battlefield, and was taken to Washington. Just before Whipple's death on May 7, President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln"), a friend of Whipple's, promoted him to major general of volunteers. Lincoln rode in an open carriage as part of Whipple's funeral cortege. He was later awarded posthumously more brevets for his wartime services, and both of his sons received presidential appointments to the military academy of their choice.[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DAB-2) He was buried in the Proprietors' Cemetery, [Portsmouth, New Hampshire](/wiki/Portsmouth,_New_Hampshire "Portsmouth, New Hampshire").

## Commemoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amiel_Weeks_Whipple&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Commemoration")]

Whipple was memorialized with the naming of two forts, [Fort Whipple](/wiki/Fort_Whipple,_Arizona "Fort Whipple, Arizona"), [Arizona Territory](/wiki/Arizona_Territory "Arizona Territory") and Fort Whipple, Arlington County, Virginia (now [Fort Myer](/wiki/Fort_Myer "Fort Myer")).[[1]](#cite_note-TSHA-1) In California, Whipple Peak was named in his honor by [Joseph C. Ives](/wiki/Joseph_C._Ives "Joseph C. Ives"), a surveyor who served with Whipple on the Mexican boundary survey.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Numerous plant taxa were named in his honor, including the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") *[Whipplea](/wiki/Whipplea "Whipplea")* and [species](/wiki/Species "Species") *[Hesperoyucca whipplei](/wiki/Hesperoyucca_whipplei "Hesperoyucca whipplei")*, and *[Sclerocactus whipplei](/wiki/Sclerocactus_whipplei "Sclerocactus whipplei")*.

The fish *[Cyprinella whipplei](/wiki/Cyprinella_whipplei "Cyprinella whipplei")* [Girard](/wiki/Charles_Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Girard "Charles Frédéric Girard"), 1856 was named after him,[[8]](#cite_note-ETYFish-8) as well as *[Etheostoma whipplei](/wiki/Redfin_darter "Redfin darter")*.

The standard [author abbreviation](/wiki/List_of_botanists_by_author_abbreviation_(A) "List of botanists by author abbreviation (A)") Whipple is used to indicate this person as the author when [citing](/wiki/Author_citation_(botany) "Author citation (botany)") a [botanical name](/wiki/Botanical_name "Botanical name").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
